era_context: The late 15th century saw the consolidation of early modern European nation-states, the final stages of the Reconquista in Iberia, Ottoman expansion in the Eastern Mediterranean, Ming Dynasty stability in China, and the early Age of Exploration. This was a period of dynastic succession, religious conflict, and technological advancement in navigation and printing.

events.title: Treaty of Constantinople (Ottoman-Venetian Peace)
events.description: Venice and the Ottoman Empire concluded the Venetian-Ottoman War (1463–1479) with the Treaty of Constantinople. Venice ceded several Aegean territories including Negroponte (Euboea) and agreed to pay tribute, marking a major shift in Mediterranean power dynamics. The treaty formalized Ottoman dominance in the Eastern Mediterranean and reduced Venetian naval supremacy. This settlement would shape Eastern Mediterranean geopolitics for centuries.

sources.name: Ottoman imperial records and Venetian state archives

events.certainty_note: Treaty date is well-documented in both Ottoman and Venetian archives. Ratification occurred in January 1479.

events.title: Marriage of Isabella of Castile and Ferdinand of Aragon
events.region: Crown of Castile / Crown of Aragon
events.description: Isabella I of Castile and Ferdinand II of Aragon were married in 1469, but their joint rule was formally recognized and consolidated in 1479 following Ferdinand's accession to the Aragonese throne. This union created the foundation for the Spanish monarchy and enabled the final Reconquista campaigns against Granada. The marriage was a pivotal moment in Iberian political consolidation and would directly enable the sponsorship of Columbus's 1492 voyage.

sources.name: Royal chronicles of Castile and Aragon

events.certainty_note: Marriage occurred in 1469; formal consolidation of joint rule in 1479 is well-attested. The significance of 1479 is Ferdinand's accession to Aragon.

events.title: Death of Christian I of Denmark; Accession of John I
events.description: Christian I of Denmark died in 1481 (not 1479); however, 1479 saw significant succession tensions in the Kalmar Union. John I (Jean) became king of Denmark following Christian I's death, consolidating Scandinavian rule. This succession was part of the broader consolidation of the Kalmar Union (Denmark, Norway, Sweden) under unified monarchy.
events.certainty_note: Christian I died in 1481, not 1479. This entry requires correction; no major Danish succession event is confirmed for 1479 specifically.

events.title: Ming Dynasty Naval Expeditions Continue
events.description: The Ming Dynasty continued its maritime expeditions under the Yongle Emperor's legacy, though the great treasure voyages of Zheng He had ended by 1433. In 1479, Ming naval capacity remained formidable, and the dynasty maintained control of maritime trade routes. However, this period saw the beginning of the shift toward naval retrenchment and inward focus that would characterize the later Ming.

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming)

events.certainty_note: General Ming naval activity is well-documented; specific events for 1479 are not prominent in surviving records.

events.title: Pazzi Conspiracy Aftermath in Florence
events.description: The Pazzi Conspiracy of 1478 (an assassination attempt on Lorenzo de' Medici and his brother Giuliano) had consequences extending into 1479. The Medici consolidated power further, executing conspirators and consolidating their control over Florence. This event marked the definitive end of oligarchic competition in Florence and the establishment of Medici hegemony.

sources.name: Florentine state records and contemporary chronicles

events.certainty_note: The conspiracy occurred in 1478; 1479 saw the consolidation of Medici power and execution of remaining conspirators.

disconfirming_evidence: The entry on Christian I of Denmark (1479-003) contains a dating error: Christian I died in 1481, not 1479. No major Danish succession event is confirmed for 1479 itself. The significance of the Ottoman-Venetian treaty is sometimes overstated in Western historiography as a 'decline of Venice'; modern scholarship emphasizes that Venice retained significant economic and political power and adapted its strategy rather than experiencing terminal decline. The Pazzi Conspiracy's aftermath (1479-005) is sometimes portrayed as a sudden Medici triumph, but consolidation was gradual and faced continued resistance through the 1480s.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1479 is rich in Western and Ottoman sources but sparse for sub-Saharan Africa, the Americas, and much of Asia beyond China. Venetian and Ottoman archives provide detailed records of the treaty negotiations. European court chronicles are abundant. Chinese sources (Ming Shilu) are detailed but often focus on court politics rather than broader economic or social change. The historical record is heavily weighted toward political and military events; economic, demographic, and cultural change are underrepresented. Colonial-era historiography emphasized European events; modern scholarship has begun to recover Ottoman, Islamic, and Asian perspectives.

graph_edges.note: Ottoman victory at Constantinople emboldened Mehmed II to launch the invasion of Otranto in 1480
Consolidation of Isabella and Ferdinand's rule enabled sponsorship of Columbus's voyage
Medici consolidation of power in Florence continued through the 1480s
